module.title: Bass & Low End
module.description: Master the low end of your music. Learn sub bass vs mid bass design, how to build the kick-bass relationship, sidechain techniques, and how to create bass sounds that hit hard on any speaker system.

lessons.title: Sub Bass vs Mid Bass: Understanding the Low End
lessons.body:
The low end of a mix — roughly everything below 250 Hz — is the part that most beginning producers get wrong. Either it's boomy and muddy, or it's thin and weak. Understanding the distinct roles of sub bass and mid bass, and designing each intentionally, is the foundation of professional low-end management.

## The Frequency Map of the Low End

| Range | Label | Character | Perceived Through |
|---|---|---|---|
| 20–60 Hz | Sub bass | Felt more than heard | Subwoofers, body |
| 60–120 Hz | Bass body | Warmth, weight | Most speakers |
| 120–250 Hz | Upper bass | Fullness, thickness | All speakers |
| 250–500 Hz | Low midrange | Presence, muddiness risk | All speakers |

**Sub bass (20–60 Hz)**: The deepest frequencies. These aren't heard so much as felt — they move air and create physical sensation at high volumes. Sub bass is what makes music feel powerful in a club. However, sub bass is essentially inaudible on laptop speakers, earbuds, and phone speakers. A mix that relies solely on sub for its low-end weight will sound thin on most consumer devices.

**Mid bass (60–200 Hz)**: This is where the low end lives for most listeners. Kick drum thump, bass guitar body, 808 weight — these are mid-bass frequencies. Most speakers can reproduce 60–200 Hz, so designing mid-bass content is critical for ensuring your low end translates across playback systems.

**Upper bass (200–400 Hz)**: The "warmth" range. Too much here sounds muddy; too little sounds thin. This range is where mud accumulates from multiple instruments piling up.

## Sub Bass: Design for Felt Impact

A sub bass sound is typically a sine wave (or near-sine wave) at very low frequency. The sine wave produces only the fundamental frequency — no harmonics — which is why it disappears on small speakers (which can't reproduce those frequencies).

Designing a sub bass:

1. **Instrument**: Sytrus or 3xOsc in FL Studio. Set oscillator to sine wave.
2. **Frequency**: At 100 BPM in A minor, your root note A is at 55 Hz. Middle A (A1 in MIDI) is 55 Hz.
3. **Envelope**: Slow attack (30–50 ms) to avoid a click at note start. Long sustain. Medium release (100–200 ms).
4. **Filter**: No filter needed for a pure sine sub — any filter coloration is counterproductive.
5. **Processing**: Route through a high-pass at 25 Hz (removes subsonic rumble that wastes headroom) and a limiter to control peaks.

**The sub visibility problem**: Since pure sine waves are inaudible on small speakers, add a subtle harmonic layer:
- Duplicate the sub channel
- Add Fruity Waveshaper (soft clip) to the duplicate at moderate drive
- The clipping adds 2nd and 3rd harmonics (at 110 Hz and 165 Hz for A1) which ARE audible on small speakers
- Blend the clipped version at -10 to -15 dB below the clean sub

Now the sub is felt on large speakers and heard on small ones.

## Mid Bass: Design for Audible Weight

Mid bass is the bass people hear on their phone speaker. It's punchy, rhythmically active, and carries melodic content. Types of mid bass:

**Electric bass sample**: Raw, organic. Load into a Sampler channel. The 60–200 Hz frequencies provide weight; the pick or pluck transients in the 500–2000 Hz range provide articulation.

**Synth bass**: Built in Sytrus or Harmor. A sawtooth wave through a low-pass filter gives a full, growling bass. The filter cutoff controls how much harmonic content bleeds through — higher cutoff = more midrange presence.

**Reese bass**: A classic electronic bass sound. Two detuned sawtooth oscillators (5–10 cents apart) create a beating/modulation effect. Heavy low-pass filter. The interference between the two oscillators creates a sound that seems to move even when sustaining a single note. Used extensively in drum and bass, bass music, and electronic genres.

**Distorted/overdriven bass**: Any bass signal run through moderate distortion gets mid-bass frequency content boosted and harmonics added. The result cuts through a mix even on small speakers.

## Layering Sub and Mid Bass

Many professional producers use two separate bass channels:

1. **Sub layer**: Sine wave, low-pass at 100–120 Hz, provides felt weight
2. **Mid layer**: Sawtooth/sample, high-pass at 60–80 Hz, provides heard presence

The sub layer is mostly felt. The mid layer is mostly heard. Together they create a bass that works across all playback systems.

The key to layering is **phase coherence**: both layers should be tuned to the same pitch and their fundamentals should be in phase. If they're out of phase, the bass will cancel and sound weaker in mono. To check: route both to a Mixer channel, add a Fruity Stereo Shaper in mono mode, and confirm the combined level doesn't drop (phase cancellation would cause it to drop dramatically).

## How to Check Your Low End

The challenge with mixing low end is that your monitoring environment greatly affects what you hear. Techniques:

**Mono check**: Collapse the mix to mono. If the bass disappears or becomes significantly thinner, you have phase problems. Fix by adjusting the tuning or phase of the bass layers.

**Check on different devices**: Listen on your studio monitors, then on headphones, then on a phone speaker or small laptop. The bass that sounds good on all three has the right sub/mid balance.

**Spectrum analyser**: Use Parametric EQ 2 or a spectrum analyser plugin on your bass channel. Watch the frequency peaks. The fundamental (root note) should be the loudest frequency. Excessive energy in the 200–400 Hz mud range should be cut.

**LUFS meter**: Heavy low end lifts LUFS readings. If your master LUFS is high but the mix doesn't sound loud, you likely have excess low-end energy eating headroom.
keyTakeaways: Sub bass (20–60 Hz) is felt physically on large systems but inaudible on small speakers — add a subtle saturated harmonic layer at -10 to -15 dB so the bass translates to all playback devices.
Layer a pure sine sub (felt) with a harmonically rich mid-bass synth or sample (heard) on separate channels, tuned to the same pitch and checked for phase coherence in mono.
Always check your low end in mono and on a small speaker — if the bass disappears or becomes thin, fix the sub/mid balance and phase relationship before any other processing.
lessons.tryThis: In Sytrus, create a pure sine sub bass at A1 (55 Hz). Play a simple 2-bar pattern. Now play that pattern through your phone speaker — notice how the sub nearly disappears. Duplicate the channel, add Fruity Waveshaper (soft clip curve) to the duplicate, blend it at -12 dB under the original. Play through the phone speaker again — the bass should now be audible. Compare the two versions on studio monitors or headphones to ensure the clean sub still has weight on better systems.

lessons.title: The Kick-Bass Relationship
lessons.body:
The kick drum and the bass occupy the same frequency territory — the low end. When both hit at the same time at full level, they either cancel each other or create a booming, muddy clash. Getting the kick and bass to coexist cleanly — each occupying its own space and time — is one of the most important and most misunderstood skills in mixing.

## Why Kick and Bass Conflict

The kick drum's fundamental frequency typically sits between 50–100 Hz, depending on the sample. A bass note in the same key also sits in that range. When they both hit on beat 1 simultaneously, you have two sound sources generating similar frequencies at the same time. Their waveforms either add together (making the hit boomy and overpowering) or partially cancel (making it thin and inconsistent).

The result: a mix that sounds great at one volume but falls apart at another, or sounds good on big speakers but terrible on small ones.

## Strategy 1: Frequency Separation (The EQ Approach)

Give the kick and bass different frequency zones:

**The kick owns 60–100 Hz**: The kick's transient weight lives in this range. Leave this range intact in the kick EQ and cut the bass here.

**The bass owns 40–60 Hz**: The sub fundamental of the bass sits below the kick's punch. High-pass the kick at 60–80 Hz so it doesn't compete in the deepest sub range.

**Practical steps**:
1. On the kick channel: Add Parametric EQ 2. High-pass at 50 Hz (steep, 24 dB/oct). This cuts the kick's deepest sub, leaving that space for the bass.
2. On the bass channel: Add Parametric EQ 2. Cut at 80–100 Hz with a narrow bell (-4 to -6 dB). This is where the kick's punch lives — notching it slightly in the bass creates space for the kick.
3. Listen to kick and bass together in mono. They should now sound punchy and clear rather than boomy.

## Strategy 2: Rhythmic Separation (Note Programming)

The cleanest approach of all: don't have the kick and bass hit at the same time.

If the kick lands on beat 1, program the bass note so it starts slightly *after* beat 1 — just a 16th note late, or even a 32nd note late. The kick punches through the silence, then the bass enters. This is common in reggae, dub, and some R&B styles.

For house and trap, where the kick and bass often do hit simultaneously, the timing offset approach doesn't work — so frequency separation and dynamics management become essential.

## Strategy 3: Sidechain Compression

Sidechain compression is a dynamics-based approach: the bass volume automatically ducks when the kick hits, then returns to full volume after the kick transient has passed. This is the industry-standard approach for genres where kick and bass hit together.

**Setting up sidechain in FL Studio**:

Method 1 — **Fruity Peak Controller**:
1. Route the kick drum to an extra Mixer output (e.g., send a copy of the kick to Mixer send 15 at 0 dB, but muted from the main output)
2. On the bass Mixer channel, right-click the Volume knob → Link to controller → Fruity Peak Controller
3. In the Peak Controller, set the source to the kick's Mixer send
4. Set Base = 100%, Volume = -4 dB (the amount of ducking), Attack = fast (1–5 ms), Release = medium (80–150 ms)

Method 2 — **Fruity Compressor with sidechain input**:
1. On the bass channel, add Fruity Compressor
2. In the sidechain routing (available in newer FL Studio versions), route the kick signal to the sidechain input
3. Set ratio 4:1–8:1, attack 1–3 ms, release 100–150 ms, threshold so the compressor engages hard on kick hits

**The release time relationship to BPM**: The release time determines how long the bass stays ducked. At 120 BPM, one quarter note = 500 ms. A release of 100–120 ms means the bass fully recovers before the next kick hit — clean and transparent. A release of 250–300 ms at 120 BPM means the bass is still slightly ducked when the next kick hits — giving a subtle pumping feel.

## Strategy 4: Transient Shaping

A transient shaper gives you direct control over the attack (transient) and sustain of any signal. On the kick, increasing the attack sharpens the punch — making it cut through rather than blob over the bass. On the bass, reducing the transient slightly softens its attack so it doesn't compete with the kick's click.

In FL Studio: add Fruity Peak Controller or a third-party transient shaper plugin on the kick channel. Increase the attack setting slightly — the transient click becomes more defined, more surgical.

## Tuning the Kick to the Key

An often-overlooked technique: tune the kick drum's fundamental frequency to match the key of the track. A kick in A minor whose fundamental is at A (55 Hz, 110 Hz, or 220 Hz) reinforces the harmonic content of the bass and chords rather than clashing with it.

In the Sampler channel, use the **Pitch** knob to tune the kick up or down in semitones. Use a spectrum analyser to identify the kick's current fundamental, then calculate how many semitones you need to shift to land on the root note of your key.

Some producers prefer a neutral kick pitch (one that doesn't obviously match any key — tuned between notes) so the kick works in any track. Experiment with both approaches.

## Practical Workflow

1. Set kick level first — this is your loudness anchor
2. Add the bass, targeting the same rough level as the kick on beats where they coincide
3. High-pass the kick at 50 Hz
4. Notch the bass at 80–100 Hz (the kick's punch zone)
5. Check in mono — if it sounds clean, add sidechain compression to the bass for additional separation
6. Adjust sidechain release until the interaction sounds natural for the genre (subtle for lo-fi, more pumping for house)
7. Check on multiple playback devices
keyTakeaways: EQ frequency separation — high-pass the kick at 50 Hz and notch the bass at 80–100 Hz — gives each element its own zone before any dynamics processing is applied.
Sidechain compression (fast attack, BPM-matched release) makes the bass duck when the kick hits — the release time determines whether the effect is transparent or contributes a pumping feel.
Tune the kick's fundamental frequency to the root note of the track's key so the kick reinforces rather than clashes with the harmonic content of the bass and chords.
lessons.tryThis: Set up a pattern with a kick on beat 1 and a bass note on beat 1. First, listen to them colliding without any processing. Then add a Parametric EQ 2 to the kick with a high-pass at 55 Hz. Add a Parametric EQ 2 to the bass with a narrow bell cut at 90 Hz (-5 dB). Listen again — notice the improvement. Finally, add sidechain compression to the bass from the kick (ratio 5:1, attack 2 ms, release 120 ms). Toggle the sidechain on and off while listening in mono. Each step should make the combination cleaner.

lessons.title: Designing Bass Sounds from Scratch
lessons.body:
Every great bass sound starts with understanding what you want it to do in the mix — felt sub weight, heard melodic presence, rhythmic grunt, or harmonic richness. Once you know the role the bass will play, you can design it specifically for that purpose rather than browsing presets until something sticks.

## The Building Blocks of Any Bass Sound

All synthesised bass sounds consist of the same core components:

1. **Oscillator waveform**: The raw sound source. Determines the harmonic content (how many overtones).
2. **Filter**: Shapes which frequencies pass through. The filter cutoff and resonance are the most expressive controls in bass synthesis.
3. **Amplitude envelope**: Controls how the volume changes over time (attack, decay, sustain, release).
4. **Pitch envelope**: Adds pitch movement over time — essential for "thump" basses and 808-style sounds.
5. **Modulation (LFO)**: Creates movement — tremolo, vibrato, filter wobble.

## Oscillator Choice for Bass

| Waveform | Harmonic Content | Bass Character |
|---|---|---|
| Sine | None (fundamental only) | Pure sub, very clean, disappears on small speakers |
| Triangle | Odd harmonics, quiet | Warm, slightly present, still relatively clean |
| Sawtooth | All harmonics | Full, buzzy, cuts through the mix, classic synth bass |
| Square | Odd harmonics only | Hollow, dark, good for Reese-style bass |
| Pulse (narrow) | Rich odd harmonics | Thin, nasal, good for aggressive bass textures |

**The practical choice**: For sub-focused bass, use sine. For mid-presence bass, use sawtooth. For everything in between, use triangle with a gentle filter.

## Three Essential Bass Designs

### 1. The Sub Bass (Pure Sine)

**In Sytrus**:
- Operator 1: Sine wave, no FM
- Volume envelope: Attack 30 ms, Decay 0 ms, Sustain 100%, Release 150 ms
- Pitch envelope: Optional slight downward curve (5–10 cents over 100 ms) for a subtle "settle" into the pitch
- No filter needed
- Output: Direct to mixer, high-pass at 25 Hz

### 2. The Mid-Bass Synth (Sawtooth + Filter)

**In 3xOsc**:
- All three oscillators: Sawtooth waves
- Oscillator 2 and 3: Detune by +7 and -7 cents respectively. This creates the "chorused" thickness of a Reese or classic synth bass.
- Parametric EQ in the Mixer: Low-pass at 800–1200 Hz (remove harsh high harmonics)
- Filter (in 3xOsc or via Parametric EQ 2): Cut above 800 Hz for a dark, round bass; leave open to 2 kHz for a brighter, more aggressive tone
- Volume envelope: Fast attack (5 ms), slow decay into sustain

### 3. The 808 / Kick-Bass Hybrid

This is the trap 808 design described in Module 13, but applicable anywhere:

**In Sytrus**:
- Operator 1: Sine wave
- Pitch envelope: Steep downward slope — pitch starts 2 octaves above the target note and drops to the target note over 50–100 ms. This is the "thump" — a fast pitch-fall that mimics the acoustic behaviour of a drum membrane.
- Volume envelope: Fast attack, long release (1–3 seconds)
- Amplitude curve: Exponential decay so the note fades naturally

The depth and speed of the pitch envelope determines the character: a fast steep drop = punchy trap 808 thump; a slower gentler drop = smoother melodic bass.

## Filter as Expression: The Cutoff Sweep

The most expressive control on any bass synth is the filter cutoff. Moving it up reveals harmonics; moving it down darkens and removes them.

**Static filter**: Set once and leave it. A low-pass cutoff at 500 Hz gives a round, dark bass. At 1500 Hz, the bass gets brighter and more midrange-present.

**Envelope-controlled filter**: Link the filter cutoff to an envelope. A fast attack on the filter envelope makes the bass "open up" on each note attack (bright transient, then darker sustain). This is the classic synth bass articulation that makes the bass feel active and alive.

**LFO-controlled filter**: A slow LFO on the filter cutoff creates a wobbling effect. At 1–2 Hz, this is a subtle pulse. At 8–16 Hz, it creates a machine-gun wub that is the foundation of dubstep and bass music.

**Manual automation**: In FL Studio's Piano Roll, right-click any synth parameter knob and Create Automation Clip. Draw the cutoff by hand — this gives full compositional control over the bass character throughout the track.

## Distortion and Saturation for Bass

Distortion on bass sounds counterintuitive but is extremely useful:

- **Soft clipping** (Fruity Soft Clipper): Adds even harmonics. Makes the bass audible on small speakers. Gentle and musical.
- **Hard clipping** (Fruity Blood Overdrive): More aggressive harmonic content. Used for growling bass sounds and aggressive genres.
- **Tape saturation** (Fruity Waveshaper with a smooth curve): Warmth and compression. Makes the bass feel glued into the mix.

Distortion on bass should be applied **in parallel**: blend the distorted signal with the clean signal so you retain the sub weight while adding harmonics. Use the Mix knob on the distortion plugin, or route to parallel channels.

## Multi-Oscillator Thickness

One oscillator at one pitch = thin. Multiple oscillators = thick. Techniques:

- **Unison/detune**: Multiple oscillators slightly detuned (3xOsc with all three oscillators) creates width and warmth without changing the apparent pitch
- **Octave layering**: A second oscillator one octave below the main oscillator adds sub weight without any sub bass design required
- **Fifth stacking**: A second oscillator tuned to the perfect fifth above the root (7 semitones) adds harmonic richness. Used in power chords and classic synth bass sounds.
keyTakeaways: Choose oscillator waveform based on the bass's role: sine for sub-only weight, sawtooth for harmonically rich mid-bass presence, triangle for a middle-ground warm tone.
A fast downward pitch envelope (pitch starting 2 octaves high and dropping in 50–100 ms) creates the click-into-thump character of 808-style and drum-hit basses.
Apply distortion in parallel — blend the distorted signal with the clean sub so you add harmonics (audibility on small speakers) without losing sub weight.
lessons.tryThis: In 3xOsc, create a sawtooth bass: all three oscillators on saw waves, oscillators 2 and 3 detuned by +8 and -8 cents. Add a Parametric EQ 2 in the Mixer and set a low-pass cutoff at 800 Hz. Listen and compare. Now automate the cutoff: create an automation clip for the EQ's first band frequency knob. Draw a curve that rises from 400 Hz to 1200 Hz over 4 bars then falls back to 400 Hz. Listen to how the bass opens and closes. This is the basic filter sweep used to create movement in electronic bass lines.

lessons.title: Bass Groove and Melodic Bass Lines
lessons.body:
A bass line that only plays root notes on the downbeat is functional but forgettable. The most memorable music in any genre — funk, soul, jazz, hip-hop, house — has bass lines that are rhythmically interesting, melodically inventive, and groovy in a way that compels movement. This lesson covers how to write and programme bass lines with real musical character.

## The Groove of the Bass

Groove in a bass line comes from the tension between where notes land relative to the beat, and how long they sustain. A note placed slightly early (ahead of the beat) feels urgent and forward-leaning. A note placed slightly late (behind the beat) feels relaxed and pocket-y. A note that's too short leaves rhythmic space; a note that sustains too long fills up all the air.

**The groove sweet spot**: In most genres, bass notes that land slightly behind the beat (5–10 ticks late in FL Studio) create a "pocket" feel — the bass seems to lock with the kick drum's sustain rather than its attack. This is the rhythm that makes people nod their heads.

## Rhythmic Patterns Beyond Root Notes

**Octave jumping**: The bass plays the root note, then jumps an octave (or back down), creating a bouncing, energetic feel. Used extensively in house, funk, and R&B.

**Syncopated riffs**: The bass anticipates the next chord change by playing its root note on the last 16th note of the bar before the change. This "push" into the next section creates momentum.

**Walking bass**: A jazz technique — the bass steps between chord roots using passing tones (chromatic and scalar notes that connect root to root). A walking bass line on a 4-chord progression might hit 16th notes continuously, creating an almost melodic through-line.

**Call and response**: The bass plays a short melodic phrase, then rests, then responds to itself with a different phrase. This conversation structure appears in funk and soul bass lines.

## Melodic Bass: Beyond the Root

Once the root note is established, the bass can play chord tones and passing notes to create melodic content:

**Chord tones available over each chord**:
- Over a minor 7 chord (Am7): A, C, E, G
- Over a major 7 chord (Fmaj7): F, A, C, E
- Use the root first, then any of the other chord tones for melodic variation

**Chromatic passing notes**: Notes that are a half step above or below the next chord tone. Approaching the root of the next chord from a half step below (a chromatic lower neighbour) is one of the most effective bass line techniques. Example: the bar before Dm7, the bass plays C# (one semitone below D) on beat 4 — then resolves to D on beat 1 of the next bar. The tension and resolution of this half-step approach is immediately musical.

**Arpeggiation**: Playing chord tones in succession rather than stacked simultaneously. A bass arpeggio over Fmaj7 might go F – A – C – E over 4 beats, outlining the chord in the bass register.

## MIDI Velocity for Bass Groove

In a live performance, bass players accent certain notes and ghost others. Replicate this in the Piano Roll:

- **Root notes and structural beats**: Velocity 95–115 (accented)
- **Passing notes and approach tones**: Velocity 60–80 (lighter touch)
- **Ghost notes**: Velocity 25–45 (barely audible, provide rhythmic texture)

The contrast between accented and ghost notes creates the groove dynamic that makes a bass line feel performed rather than programmed.

## Note Length as Articulation

Note length in the Piano Roll directly controls articulation:

- **Staccato** (note length = 1/4 of the beat): Creates a clipped, percussive quality. Good for funky bass lines with lots of rhythmic activity.
- **Normal** (note length = 1/2 to 3/4 of the beat): The most common length — punchy without being clipped. Sounds like a plucked bass.
- **Legato** (overlapping notes): Sustained, flowing. Good for melodic bass passages. Notes that overlap slightly create a smooth legato transition.
- **Portamento between notes**: When legato notes overlap in a synth with portamento enabled, the pitch glides between notes — this is the sliding bass sound common in soul, funk, and trap.

## Programming a Funky Bass Line

Funk bass is built on the interplay between what the kick drum plays and what the bass plays:

1. **Identify the kick pattern first**: Know which beats the kick hits.
2. **Lock to the kick**: Place bass notes on the same beats as the kick (creating unity) and in between the kick hits (creating contrast).
3. **Anticipate and delay**: Use anticipation (early notes just before the beat) and delay (late notes just after the beat) to create rhythmic pocket.
4. **Add passing tones**: Chromatic and scalar approach notes between chord root hits.

**Example** — at 95 BPM in A minor:
- Beat 1: A1 (locked with kick) velocity 110
- Beat 1.5: G1 velocity 55 (ghost)
- Beat 2: A1 velocity 90
- Beat 2.5: C2 velocity 65 (chord tone)
- Beat 3: A1 (locked with kick) velocity 110
- Beat 3.75: G#1 velocity 70 (chromatic approach to A)
- Beat 4: A1 velocity 90
- Beat 4.5: E2 velocity 60 (fifth — passing up to next bar)
keyTakeaways: Placing bass notes slightly behind the beat (5–10 ticks late) creates the pocket feel that locks bass to the kick drum's sustain rather than its attack transient.
Use chromatic approach notes (a half step below the root of the next chord) on beat 4 to create tension that resolves on beat 1 of the next bar — this is the most effective melodic bass technique.
Match note length to articulation intent: staccato for funky rhythmic clipping, normal (1/2–3/4 length) for plucked feel, legato with portamento for sliding melodic bass.
lessons.tryThis: Programme a 2-bar bass line in A minor at 95 BPM using the example above. Now compare two versions: one with all notes starting exactly on the grid, and one with every note moved 6 ticks later than the grid. Listen to both at the same tempo — the slightly late version should feel more relaxed and groovy. Then add a chromatic approach note: on beat 4 of bar 2, change the A1 to G#1 (one semitone below A). Listen to the tension it creates and the resolution when bar 1 repeats.
